summary: Retune Azure's managed database and cache services: SQL Database, MySQL and PostgreSQL Flexible Server, Redis Cache, and Cosmos DB. Compute and storage changes are scoped to same-family resizes and grow-only storage; anything touching replication, consistency, or network reachability stays engineer-authored.

operations.title: Update database tags
operations.summary: Set or update this database's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone. No effect on how it runs.
operations.description: Sets or merges cost-allocation and ownership tags on the SQL database (tags). Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on the running database or its data.

params.name: database
params.label: SQL database
params.help: The database to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Avoid < > % & ? / in tag names — Azure rejects them.

operations.title: Resize the compute and storage tier
operations.summary: Change the database's service tier and vCore count. Applying causes a brief connection drop while Azure cuts over to the new size.
operations.description: Sets sku_name to a different General Purpose (vCore, Gen5) size. Azure SQL Database applies this online: it provisions the new compute size in parallel, then cuts existing connections over — typically a few seconds, occasionally longer if a long-running transaction has to unwind. Scoped to one purchasing model (General Purpose vCore, Gen5) so this is a straightforward resize, not a purchasing-model switch or a Hyperscale/Business Critical migration.

params.name: database
params.label: SQL database
params.help: The database to resize.

params.name: new_sku_name
params.label: New size
params.help: General Purpose (vCore, Gen5) size to move to. More vCores cost more and add compute headroom; fewer vCores cost less.

decisions: Curated to the General Purpose Gen5 vCore family only — moving to Business Critical, Hyperscale, or the DTU model is a bigger operation and stays engineer-authored.
A few seconds of dropped connections is typical during cutover; a long-running transaction in flight can extend that.

operations.title: Grow maximum database size
operations.summary: Grow this database's maximum storage size. Storage can only increase, never shrink.
operations.description: Grows max_size_gb, the ceiling on how much data the database can hold. Applies online with no interruption; Azure enforces that this value can only increase for a given database.

params.name: database
params.label: SQL database
params.help: The database to grow.

params.name: new_max_size_gb
params.label: New maximum size (GB)
params.help: Must be greater than the current maximum size; Azure SQL Database storage ceilings cannot be reduced. Capped here at 4096 GB — larger jumps route to an engineer.

operations.title: Change point-in-time restore retention
operations.summary: Change how many days of automatic backups this database keeps for point-in-time restore.
operations.description: Updates short_term_retention_policy.retention_days, the window Azure keeps automatic backups available for point-in-time restore. Applies online with no interruption.

params.name: database
params.label: SQL database
params.help: The database to retune.

params.name: retention_days
params.label: Retention (days)
params.help: Days of point-in-time restore backups to keep (Azure allows 1-35).

operations.title: Update server tags
operations.summary: Set or update this server's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone. No effect on how it runs.

params.name: database

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Avoid < > % & ? / in tag names — Azure rejects them.

operations.title: Resize the compute tier
operations.summary: Change the server's compute tier and size. Applying restarts the server, typically for 60 to 120 seconds.
operations.description: Sets sku_name to a different General Purpose (Standard, Ddsv4) size. Unlike a storage change, a compute tier or size change on MySQL Flexible Server restarts the server — documented as roughly 60 to 120 seconds of unavailability while it comes back up on the new size.

params.name: database
params.label: MySQL server
params.help: The server to resize.

params.name: new_sku_name
params.label: New size
params.help: General Purpose (Standard, Ddsv4) size to move to. Bigger sizes cost more and add compute headroom; smaller sizes cost less.

decisions: Compute scaling restarts the server for roughly 60-120 seconds, so this stays engineer-authored rather than self-service.
Curated to the General Purpose Ddsv4 family; moving to Burstable or Memory Optimized is a bigger change and stays engineer-authored too.

operations.title: Change how many days of backups are kept
operations.summary: Change how many days of automatic backups this server keeps.
operations.description: Updates backup_retention_days. Applies online with no restart.

params.name: database
params.label: MySQL server
params.help: The server to retune.

params.name: new_backup_retention_days
params.label: New retention period (days)
params.help: Days of automated backups to retain (Azure allows 1-35).

operations.title: Grow server storage
operations.summary: Grow this server's storage. Storage can only increase, never shrink.
operations.description: Grows storage.size_gb. Applies online with no restart; Azure enforces that storage can only increase for a given server.

params.name: database
params.label: MySQL server
params.help: The server to grow.

params.name: new_size_gb
params.label: New storage size (GB)
params.help: Must be greater than the current storage size; MySQL Flexible Server storage cannot be reduced. Capped here at 4096 GB — larger jumps route to an engineer.

operations.title: Update server tags
operations.summary: Set or update this server's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone. No effect on how it runs.
operations.description: Sets or merges cost-allocation and ownership tags on the PostgreSQL Flexible Server (tags). Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on the running server or its data.

params.name: database
params.label: PostgreSQL server
params.help: The server to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Avoid < > % & ? / in tag names — Azure rejects them.

operations.title: Resize the compute tier
operations.summary: Change the server's compute tier and vCore count. Applying restarts the server.
operations.description: Sets sku_name to a different General Purpose (Standard, Dsv3) size. PostgreSQL Flexible Server rebuilds the server at the new size and cuts over; Azure's near-zero-downtime scaling usually keeps this under 30 seconds, but a regular restart can take several minutes, and near-zero-downtime scaling does not apply to every configuration (unlogged tables lose their data on cutover; the smallest Burstable sizes are excluded).

params.name: database
params.label: PostgreSQL server
params.help: The server to resize.

params.name: new_sku_name
params.label: New size
params.help: General Purpose (Standard, Dsv3) size to move to. Bigger sizes cost more and add compute headroom; smaller sizes cost less.

decisions: Compute scaling restarts the server, so this stays engineer-authored rather than self-service — worth confirming the target has no unlogged tables before applying, since those lose their data on cutover.
Curated to the General Purpose Dsv3 family; moving to Burstable or Memory Optimized is a bigger change and stays engineer-authored too.

operations.title: Grow server storage
operations.summary: Grow this server's storage. Storage can only increase, never shrink.
operations.description: Grows storage_mb. Applies online with no restart; Azure enforces that storage can only increase for a given server.

params.name: database
params.label: PostgreSQL server
params.help: The server to grow.

params.name: new_storage_mb
params.label: New storage size (MB)
params.help: Must be greater than the current storage size, in MB (32768 MB = 32 GiB). PostgreSQL Flexible Server storage cannot be reduced. Capped here at 4194304 MB (4 TiB) — larger jumps route to an engineer.

operations.title: Change how many days of backups are kept
operations.summary: Change how many days of automatic backups this server keeps.
operations.description: Updates backup_retention_days. Applies online with no restart.

params.name: database

params.help: The server to retune.

params.name: new_backup_retention_days
params.label: New retention period (days)
params.help: Days of automated backups to retain (Azure allows 7-35).

operations.title: Update cache tags
operations.summary: Set or update this cache's tags. Only the tags you list change; others are left alone. No effect on how it runs.
operations.description: Sets or merges cost-allocation and ownership tags on the Redis cache (tags). Non-disruptive, takes effect immediately, no impact on the running cache or its data.

params.name: database
params.label: Redis cache
params.help: The cache to tag.

params.name: tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to set; existing keys not listed here are left untouched. Avoid < > % & ? / in tag names — Azure rejects them.

decisions: Capacity and SKU/family changes restart the cache and can briefly drop connections, and the valid capacity range depends on which family/SKU it is paired with — that coupling and the reboot risk keep sizing changes engineer-authored rather than self-service.

operations.title: Provision a new SQL server
operations.summary: Request a new SQL server; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies. Credentials are never entered here.
operations.description: Stands up an Azure SQL logical server as a complete specification: identity, region, API version, admin login and network posture. minimum_tls_version is fixed at 1.2 and public_network_access_enabled is fixed off for every new server; the admin password is never entered here — the engineer wires it through the secrets manager. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: server_name
params.label: Server name
params.help: Globally unique lowercase name Azure uses for the server's DNS endpoint.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this belongs to.

params.name: server_version
params.label: Server API version
params.help: Azure SQL server API version — 12.0 is the current generation and the only supported value for new servers.

params.name: administrator_login
params.label: Admin login
params.help: Server-level admin login name. The matching password is never entered here.

params.name: administrator_login_password
params.label: Admin password
params.help: Never entered here — the engineer sets this through the secrets manager during review.

params.name: minimum_tls_version
params.label: Minimum TLS version
params.help: Always enforced at 1.2 for new servers — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: Off by default so the server has no public endpoint. Choosing On exposes a public endpoint; pick On only with a reason a reviewer will read — most workloads should use private (VNet) connectivity, set up by the engineer during review.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this holds
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM — what this server holds.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this server.

decisions: Master password goes through the secrets manager — never entered in this portal
Azure AD-only authentication versus SQL authentication is an engineer decision during review
No firewall rule is created here, so nothing can reach this server until the engineer adds one or wires up private connectivity
Transparent data encryption key management (service-managed versus a customer key) is an engineer decision during review

operations.title: Provision a new SQL database
operations.summary: Request a brand-new SQL database on an existing server; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stands up an Azure SQL database on an existing logical server as a complete specification: identity, size (sku_name) and maximum size (max_size_gb). Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review.

params.name: database_name
params.label: Database name
params.help: Name of the database within the server.

params.name: sql_server
params.label: SQL server
params.help: Which SQL server this database is created on.

params.name: sku_name
params.label: Size (SKU)
params.help: General Purpose (vCore, Gen5) size — the same list the resize action offers.

params.name: max_size_gb
params.label: Maximum size (GB)
params.help: Maximum size ceiling for this database, in GB. Can grow later; the resize action never lets it shrink.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this holds
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM — what this database holds.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this database.

decisions: Collation and elastic pool placement are engineer decisions during review
Point-in-time and long-term backup retention are tuned separately after the database exists
Read scale and zone redundancy are engineer decisions during review

operations.title: Provision a new PostgreSQL flexible server
operations.summary: Request a new PostgreSQL server; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies. Credentials are never entered here.
operations.description: Stands up a PostgreSQL Flexible Server as a complete specification: identity, region, version, size (sku_name) and storage (storage_mb). public_network_access_enabled defaults off; choosing to enable it needs a reason a reviewer will read. The admin password is never entered here — the engineer wires it through the secrets manager. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: server_name

params.help: PostgreSQL major version to run.

params.name: sku_name
params.label: Size (SKU)
params.help: General Purpose (Standard, Dsv3) size — the same list the resize action offers.

params.name: storage_mb
params.label: Storage (MB)
params.help: Storage size in MB (32768 MB = 32 GiB). Can grow later; the resize action never lets it shrink.

params.name: administrator_login
params.label: Admin login
params.help: Server-level admin login name. The matching password is never entered here.

params.name: administrator_password
params.label: Admin password
params.help: Never entered here — the engineer sets this through the secrets manager during review.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: Off by default so the server has no public endpoint. Choosing On needs a reason a reviewer will read — most workloads should use private (VNet-integrated) networking instead, set up by the engineer during review.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this holds

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge

decisions: Master password goes through the secrets manager — never entered in this portal

VNet integration (a delegated subnet) is set up separately when private networking is required
Backup retention and geo-redundant backup are tuned after the server exists

operations.title: Provision a new Cosmos DB account

operations.description: Stands up a Cosmos DB account as a complete specification: identity, region, offer_type, consistency_policy and a first geo_location entry. public_network_access_enabled defaults off; choosing On needs a reason a reviewer will read. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — additional regions and the API kind are engineer decisions during review.

params.name: account_name

params.help: Globally unique lowercase name Azure uses for the account's endpoint.

params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this belongs to.

params.name: offer_type
params.label: Offer type
params.help: Azure's only offer type today; kept fixed since no alternative exists.

params.name: consistency_level
params.label: Default consistency level
params.help: Default consistency guarantee for reads across the account. Session balances consistency and latency for most workloads.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: Off by default so the account has no public endpoint. Choosing On needs a reason a reviewer will read — most workloads should use a private endpoint instead, set up by the engineer during review.

params.help: Who to call about this account.

decisions: Additional geo-replication regions, multi-region writes and automatic failover are engineer decisions during review
API kind (SQL, MongoDB, Cassandra and so on) defaults to the SQL API; changing it is an engineer decision during review
Backup mode and retention are tuned separately after the account exists

operations.title: Provision a new Redis cache
operations.summary: Request a brand-new Redis cache; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stands up a Redis cache as a complete specification: identity, region, sku_name, family and capacity. minimum_tls_version is fixed at 1.2 and the non-TLS port stays off for every new cache; public_network_access_enabled defaults off. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: cache_name
params.label: Cache name
params.help: Globally unique lowercase name Azure uses for the cache's DNS endpoint.

params.name: resource_group
params.label: Resource group
params.help: The resource group this belongs to.

params.name: sku_name
params.label: Tier (SKU)
params.help: Standard adds replication over Basic (not offered here — no SLA); Premium adds clustering, persistence and VNet options. Family and capacity below must match.

params.name: family
params.label: Family
params.help: C pairs with the Standard tier; P pairs with Premium. Must match the tier chosen above.

params.name: capacity
params.label: Capacity
params.help: Size within the family — higher numbers add memory and throughput. This range is valid for both the C (Standard) and P (Premium) families.

params.name: minimum_tls_version
params.label: Minimum TLS version
params.help: Always enforced at 1.2 for new caches — written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: non_ssl_port_enabled
params.label: Non-TLS port
params.help: Always off for new caches — the non-TLS 6379 port is never opened, written into the draft, never asked.

params.name: public_network_access_enabled
params.label: Public network access
params.help: Off by default so the cache has no public endpoint. Choosing On needs a reason a reviewer will read — most workloads should use a private endpoint instead, set up by the engineer during review.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this holds
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM — what this cache holds.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this cache.

decisions: Clustering (shard count), persistence (RDB or AOF) and Redis modules are engineer decisions during review
Patch schedule and maintenance window are tuned separately after the cache exists
